context.label: In Possession Phase
context.name: IN_POSSESSION
context.definition: In Possession phase starts when a team has the ball in a zone at the back (GK, FB or CB zone) for at least 4 seconds. The possession phase ends when the opponent wins the ball, there is a second ball, a set-piece follows, or there is any other interruption in play.
context.meaning: Controlled build-up of an attack.

parentKpi.label: Assists
parentKpi.name: ASSISTS
parentKpi.definition:
There are four types of assists:

1) Direct Assist: A pass to a teammate counts as an assist if the teammate scores immediately afterward.

2) Deflected or Blocked Actions: An action by a player (shot, pass, or cross) is counted as an assist if it is deflected or blocked by the goalkeeper or an opponent, and a teammate subsequently scores.

3) Won Fouls: A foul drawn by a player counts as an assist if it leads to a converted penalty or a directly scored free kick.

4) Forced Own Goals: Actions that directly result in an opponent’s own goal are also counted as assists.
parentKpi.meaning: Creating goals, playing the final pass.

context.label: Attacking Transition Phase
context.name: ATTACKING_TRANSITION
context.definition: Attacking Transition phases of play start when possession of the ball is won where there has been some control of the ball (pressure <40). It ends as soon as the team decides to take speed out of the situation (see possession phase), there is a second ball, a set-piece follows or there is any other interruption to play.
context.meaning: Direct attack after winning the ball without having brought the ball under complete control.

parentKpi.label: Assists
parentKpi.name: ASSISTS
parentKpi.definition:
There are four types of assists:

1) Direct Assist: A pass to a teammate counts as an assist if the teammate scores immediately afterward.

2) Deflected or Blocked Actions: An action by a player (shot, pass, or cross) is counted as an assist if it is deflected or blocked by the goalkeeper or an opponent, and a teammate subsequently scores.

3) Won Fouls: A foul drawn by a player counts as an assist if it leads to a converted penalty or a directly scored free kick.

4) Forced Own Goals: Actions that directly result in an opponent’s own goal are also counted as assists.
parentKpi.meaning: Creating goals, playing the final pass.

context.label: Set Piece Phase
context.name: SET_PIECE
context.definition: Set piece phases start with the taking of a set piece (throw-in, corner, free kick, goalkeeper kick, penalty kick). While throw-ins, penalty kicks and goalkeeper kicks end after being taken, the set piece phase for corners and free kicks runs until an action with little opponent pressure takes place or the ball has been transported out of the final third. A direct header after a corner therefore also falls into the standard phase.
context.meaning: Attack through a set piece, especially after free kicks and corners.

parentKpi.label: Assists
parentKpi.name: ASSISTS
parentKpi.definition:
There are four types of assists:

1) Direct Assist: A pass to a teammate counts as an assist if the teammate scores immediately afterward.

2) Deflected or Blocked Actions: An action by a player (shot, pass, or cross) is counted as an assist if it is deflected or blocked by the goalkeeper or an opponent, and a teammate subsequently scores.

3) Won Fouls: A foul drawn by a player counts as an assist if it leads to a converted penalty or a directly scored free kick.

4) Forced Own Goals: Actions that directly result in an opponent’s own goal are also counted as assists.
parentKpi.meaning: Creating goals, playing the final pass.

context.label: Second Ball Phase
context.name: SECOND_BALL
context.definition: Second Ball phase of the game begins after uncontrollable or extremely hard to control actions, e.g. an aerial duel or blocked pass/shot, when both teams are then in a fight for the ball. You cannot clearly assign possession of the ball to either of the two teams in these phases. The phase ends when one team manages to bring enough control over the ball (pressure <40) or there is a stoppage of play.
context.meaning: Phase in which neither team manages to keep full control of the ball. 'Chaos phase.'

details.label: Bypassed Opponents Raw
details.definition: Bypassed opponents without receiving pressure and without verification by the pass receiver. The analysis takes place between a player's ball reception or set piece and the subsequent ball reception.
details.meaning: The pure number of bypassed opponents.

details.label: Bypassed Defenders Raw
details.definition: Bypassed defenders without receiving pressure and without verification by the pass receiver. The analysis takes place between a player's ball reception or set piece and the subsequent ball reception.
details.meaning: The pure number of bypassed defenders.

details.label: Post-Shot xG
details.definition: Post-shot xG is a value of all shots on target (all shots on goal that go in or are saved by the goalkeeper). In addition to the shot-based xG variables (shot distance, shot angle, pressure on the shooter, opponents between ball and goal, phase of play, and type of shot), the "likely finishing position" of the ball as it crosses the goal line is also used to create the post-shot xG value. All shots that are blocked, hit the post, crossbar or go out of bounds are not taken into account and are given a value of 0.
details.meaning: Post-shot xG tells us how high the goal probability of a shot is, taking into account all pre-shot variables and the placement of the shot. A comparison of post-shot xG and shot-based xG can allow you to make a judgement about the finishing efficiency of a striker. A comparison of (conceded) post-shot xG and (conceded) goals can allow you to make a judgement about the goalkeepers shot stopping performance. While shot-based xG provides information about the quality of chances, post-shot xG provides more information about the quality of the shot.

details.label: Passes
details.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat through passes. This includes all progressive passes (increasing the goal threat), regressive passes (passes that reach the teammate but reduce the goal threat) and missed passes (passes intercepted by the opponent, that go out of play or end up offside). These 3 types of passes can also be viewed separately via the KPIs PXT_PASS_PRO, PXT_PASS_REG and PXT_PASS_FAIL.
details.meaning: Changing your goal threat through passing.

details.label: Dribbles
details.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat with dribbling. This includes all progressive dribbles (increasing the goal threat), regressive dribbles (dribbles without losing the ball but still reduce the goal threat) and unsuccessful dribbles (dribbles that end up with the ball being lost to the opponent or go out of play or when the dribbling player commits a foul themselves). These 3 types of dribbles can also be viewed separately via the KPIs PXT_DRIBBLE_PRO, PXT_DRIBBLE_REG and PXT_DRIBBLE_FAIL.
details.meaning: Changing your goal threat through dribbling.

details.label: Set pieces
details.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat through set-pieces (free kick, corner, throw-in, goal kick, kick-off). This includes all progressive set-pieces (increasing the goal threat), regressive set-pieces (passes that reach a teammate but reduce goal threat) and set-pieces that end up with possession lost to the opponent or offside. These 3 types of set-pieces can also be viewed separately via the KPIs PXT_SETPIECE_PRO, PXT_SETPIECE_REG and PXT_SETPIECE_FAIL.
details.meaning: Changing your goal threat through set-pieces.

details.label: Blocks
details.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat due to blocked passes, shots and set-pieces. The change is calculated from the difference between the goal threat after the block and the goal threat before the block.
details.meaning: Changing your goal threat through blocked passes, shots and set-pieces.

details.label: Shots
details.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat through shots. A distinction is made whether the shot was on target or not. The reason for this is that the pxT model is based on post-shot xG: If the shot is on target, PXT_SHOT is calculated by the difference between post-shot xG and the goal threat immediately before the shot. If the shot is not on target (if it is blocked or does not hit the target), PXT_SHOT is calculated by the difference between the goal threat after the shot and the goal threat before the shot.
details.meaning: Changing your goal threat through shots.

details.label: Ball wins
details.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat through ball wins from open play. Calculated from the difference between a team’s goal threat after ball win and the threat of the team before the ball win.
details.meaning: Changing your goal threat through ball wins.

details.label: Fouls
details.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat due to committing fouls. The change is calculated by taking the difference between a team’s goal threat before a foul is committed and the subsequent goal threat at the opponent's free kick.
details.meaning: Changing your goal threat from fouls.

details.label: No Video
details.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat that you don´t see due to missing video. In addition, changes that cannot be directly assigned to one of your own players or to the generic team count. This includes, for example, the increase in goal threat that comes from having a kick-off. It also includes changes due to referee induced stoppages.
details.meaning: Changing your goal threat when video footage is missing.

details.definition: The increase in a team’s goal threat by receiving the ball (only positive changes). PXT_REC is not a direct part of the pxT model, because in the pxT model the passer or the set-piece taking player gets the credit for the successful pass/set-piece. We created this KPI anyway so that you can split the increase in goal threat between passer/pass receiver depending on your preference. PXT_REC is therefore the counterpart to PXT_PASS_PRO and PXT_SETPIECE_PRO.
details.meaning: Increasing your goal threat through receiving.

details.label: Passes (Opponent Threat)
details.definition: Changing the opponent's goal threat through your own passing game. Progressive passing reduces the opponent's goal threat, while regressive passing and mispassing (usually) increases the opponent's goal threat.
details.meaning: Changing the opponent's goal threat through passing.

details.label: Dribbles (Opponent Threat)
details.definition: The change in the opponent's goal threat through own dribbling. Progressive dribbling reduces the opponent's goal threat, regressive dribbling and dribbling that ends in a loss of the ball (usually) increases the opponent's goal threat.
details.meaning: Changing the opponent's goal threat through dribbling.

details.label: Set pieces (Opponent Threat)
details.definition: The increase of the goal threat through a ball reception (only positive changes). PXT_REC is not a direct part of the pxT model, because in the pxT model the passer or the set-piece executing player gets the credit for the successful pass/set-piece. We created this KPI anyway so that you can split the increase in goal threat between passer/pass receiver depending on your preference. PXT_REC is therefore the counterpart to PXT_PASS_PRO and PXT_SETPIECE_PRO.
details.meaning: Changing the opponent's goal threat through set-pieces.

details.label: Blocks (Opponent Threat)
details.definition: The change in the opponent's goal threat due to blocked passes, shots and set-pieces. The change is calculated from the difference between the opponent's goal threat after the block and the opponent's goal threat before the block.
details.meaning: Changing the opponent's goal threat through blocked passes, shots and set-pieces.

details.label: Shots (Opponent Threat)
details.definition: The change in the opponent's goal threat due to a goal kick. The difference between the opponent's goal threat immediately before the shot and the opponent's goal threat after the shot.
details.meaning: The change to the opponent's goal threat through goal kicks.

details.label: Ball wins (Opponent Threat)
details.definition: The change in the opponent's goal threat due to own ball conquests. The difference between the opponent's goal threat before the ball is won and the opponent's goal threat after the ball is won.
details.meaning: Changing the opponent's goal threat by winning the ball.

details.label: Fouls (Opponent Threat)
details.definition: The change in the opponent's goal threat due to own fouls. The change is calculated by taking the difference between the opponent's goal threat before the foul and the subsequent opponent's goal threat from an opponent's free kick.
details.meaning: Changes in the opponent's goal threat due to own fouls.

details.label: No Video (Opponent Threat)
details.definition: The change in the opponent's goal threat that you do not see in the video. In addition, changes that cannot be directly assigned to a player or the generic team count. This includes, for example, the increase in goal threat that comes from having a kick-off. It also includes changes due to referee inducedstoppages.
details.meaning: Change in opponent's goal threat when video footage is missing.

details.label: Ball losses (Opponent Threat)
details.definition: The change in the opponent's goal threat due to own ball losses. The difference between the opponent's goal threat before losing the ball and the opponent's goal threat after losing the ball.
details.meaning: Changing the opponent's goal threat by losing the ball.

details.label: Progressive passes
details.definition: Sub-KPI of PXT_PASS. The positive change in a team’s goal threat through passing. Every pass that increases a team’s goal threat counts here. This also includes passes that end with the opponent if a team’s goal threat is higher after the pass than before the pass.
details.meaning: Increasing goal threat through progressive passing.

details.label: Regressive passes
details.definition: Sub-KPI of PXT_PASS. The negative change in a team’s goal threat due to passing to a teammate. Every pass that lowers your goal threat and ends up with a teammate counts here.
details.meaning: Reducing goal threat through regressive passing.

details.label: Failed passes
details.definition: Sub-KPI of PXT_PASS. The negative change in goal threat due to passing to the opponent or offside. Every pass that lowers the goal threat and ends with the opponent/offside is counted here.
details.meaning: Reducing of goal threat due to misplaced passes.

details.label: Progressive dribbles
details.definition: Sub-KPI of PXT_DRIBBLE. The positive change in one´s goal-goal threat through dribbling. Every dribbling that increases your goal-goal threat counts here.
details.meaning: Increase goal threat through progressive dribbling.

details.label: Regressive dribbles
details.definition: Sub-KPI of PXT_DRIBBLE. The negative change in one´s own threat of scoring through dribbling without losing the ball. Every dribble that was successful and lowers the goal threat counts towards this.
details.meaning: Reduce goal threat through regressive dribbling.

details.label: Failed dribbles
details.definition: Sub-KPI of PXT_DRIBBLE. The negative change in goal threat due to failed dribbles. This includes dribbles that end up with the opponent or out of play.
details.meaning: Reduction of goal threat due to failed dribbles.

details.label: Progressive set pieces
details.definition: Sub-KPI of PXT_SETPIECE. The positive change in one´s goal threat through set-pieces. Every set-piece that increases your goal-goal threat counts here. This also includes set-pieces that end with the opponent if the goal threat is higher after the pass than before the pass.
details.meaning: Increase goal threat through progressive set-pieces.

details.label: Regressive set pieces
details.definition: Sub-KPI of PXT_SETPIECE. The negative change of a team’s goal threat through set-pieces to the teammate. Every set-piece that lowers the goal threat and ends with the teammate is counted here.
details.meaning: Reduce goal threat through regressive set-pieces.

details.label: Failed set pieces
details.definition: Sub-KPI of PXT_PASS. The negative change in goal threat due to set-pieces to the opponent or offside. Any set-piece that lowers the goal threat and ends at the opponent/offside is included here.
details.meaning: Reducing your goal threat through set-pieces that end up with the opponent.

details.label: Neutral Passes
details.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
details.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

details.label: On-Ball Time
details.definition: Effective match duration in seconds (including injury time). The time is stopped when the game is interrupted or the ball is out of play.
details.meaning: Playing time in seconds.

details.label: Goals
details.definition: Goal
details.meaning: Goal

details.label: Post-Shot xG
details.definition: Post-shot xG is a value of all shots on target (all shots on goal that go in or are saved by the goalkeeper). In addition to the shot-based xG variables (shot distance, shot angle, pressure on the shooter, opponents between ball and goal, phase of play, and type of shot), the "likely finishing position" of the ball as it crosses the goal line is also used to create the post-shot xG value. All shots that are blocked, hit the post, crossbar or go out of bounds are not taken into account and are given a value of 0.
details.meaning: Post-shot xG tells us how high the goal probability of a shot is, taking into account all pre-shot variables and the placement of the shot. A comparison of post-shot xG and shot-based xG can allow you to make a judgement about the finishing efficiency of a striker. A comparison of (conceded) post-shot xG and (conceded) goals can allow you to make a judgement about the goalkeepers shot stopping performance. While shot-based xG provides information about the quality of chances, post-shot xG provides more information about the quality of the shot.

details.label: Shot-based xG
details.definition: Shot-based expected goals (xG) is a figure that adds a value to all shot attempts. This includes shots from open-play, direct free kicks and penalty kicks. In order to generate a figure that values probability of a goal being scored from a shot, parameters such as shot distance, shot angle, pressure on the player, opponent between the ball and the goal, body part when the ball is released from and the respective match situation are taken into account.
details.meaning: Shot-based xG tell how high the scoring probability of a goal shot is, taking into account many parameters. At player and team level, the shot-based xG indicate how many goals would have been statistically expected for the player or team on the basis of the goals scored. The comparison with the goals actually scored allows a statement to be made about the finishing efficiency. The same statement can be made about the defence by comparing the opponent's xG with the goals conceded.

details.label: Packing non-shot-based xG
details.definition: Packing xG (pxG) include not only include shots on goal but also all potential shooting positions, even if no shot on goal is taken. In contrast to the shot-based xG, the packing xG goes one step further by attributing a value to these positions & does not rely on a shot being taken. A team is therefore also credited for getting into areas despite not getting shots off. E.g. if a player decides to dribble and loses the ball or attempts a pass that does not reach a teammate, this will still be attributed a pxG value. In order to put a value on the probability of a goal from the position, parameters such as distance to the goal, angle to the goal, pressure on the player, opponent between the ball and the goal, body part when the ball is released from and the respective phase of the game are taken into account. Thus, packing xG are a form of non-shot xG that takes into account the number of opponents between the ball and the goal.
details.meaning: Packing xG tell us how high the goal probability of a certain potential goal-scoring position is, taking into account various parameters. It is not important whether a shot is actually attempted. At player and team level, the packing xG tell us how many goals would have been statistically expected based on historical records of the goals scored in the given scoring situations. The comparison of the pxG and the goals actually scored allows a comparison to be made about the efficiency and luck of a team/player, based on if they are over or under performing this value.

details.label: Total Shots On Target
details.definition: A shot on target is considered to be any ball playing action where the player's intention is to score a goal and the ball ends up saved/caught by the opponent goalkeeper or in the goal.
details.meaning: Number of shots On Target.

details.label: Total Shots Successful
details.definition: A successful shot is considered to be any ball playing action where the player's intention is to score a goal and the ball ends up in the opponent goal.
details.meaning: Number of successful shots (resulting in goals).

details.label: Total Shots Saved
details.definition: A saved shot is considered to be any ball playing action where the player's intention is to score a goal and the ball ends up saved by the opponent goalkeeper.
details.meaning: Number of saved shots.

details.label: Total Shots Caught
details.definition: A caught shot is considered to be any ball playing action where the player's intention is to score a goal and the ball ends up caught by the opponent goalkeeper.
details.meaning: Number of caught shots.

details.label: Total Shots Blocked
details.definition: A blocked shot is considered to be any ball playing action where the player's intention is to score a goal and the ball ends up blocked by the opponent field player.
details.meaning: Number of blocked shots.

details.label: Total Shots Other
details.definition: A 'total shot other' is considered to be any ball playing action where the player's intention is to score a goal and the ball ends up picked up by an opponent field player (also after hitting the post).
details.meaning: Number of shots picked up by an opponent field player (including post hits)

details.label: Opponent passes (Defensive Threat)
details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding through passes from the opponent (progressive passes and regressive passes). The change in the threat of conceding is distributed to the responsible players based on the responsability model. The responsible players are rewarded for regressive passes by the opponent and penalized for progressive passes.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by opponent passes.

details.label: Opponent dribbles (Defensive Threat)
details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding through dribbles from the opponent (progressive dribbles and regressive dribbles). The change in the threat of conceding is distributed to the responsible players based on the responsability model. The responsible players are rewarded for regressive dribbles by the opponent and penalized for progressive dribbles.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by opponent dribbles.

details.label: Opponent set pieces (Defensive Threat)
details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding through set pieces from the opponent (progressive set pieces and set regressive pieces). The change in the threat of conceding is distributed to the responsible players based on the responsability model. The responsible players are rewarded for regressive set pieces by the opponent and penalized for progressive set pieces. There are two variants of special set pieces, which are distributed evenly rather than according to the responsibility model: Corner kicks that end up with a teammate and free kicks that are successfully played into the opponent's box.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by opponent set pieces.

details.label: Opponent blocks (Defensive Threat)
details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding through blocks from the opponent (progressive blocks and regressive blocks). The change in the threat of conceding is distributed to the responsible players based on the responsability model. The responsible players are rewarded for regressive blocks by the opponent and penalized for progressive blocks.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by opponent blocks.

details.label: Opponent shots (Defensive Threat)
details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding through shots from the opponent. The change in the threat of conceding is distributed equally across all players on the field and we recommend not to assign this value to the players.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by opponent shots.

details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding by winning the ball actively.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by ball wins (active KPI).

details.label: No video (Defensive Threat)
details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding due to an event that is not captured on video.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by no video events. This KPI should not be attributed to players.

details.label: Ball losses (Defensive Threat)
details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding by losing the ball actively.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by ball losses (active KPI).

details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding due to a foul.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by fouls. The highest values are achived by fouling within the own box.

details.label: Passive
details.definition: The change in a team's goal threat while the opponent team is in ball possession. The change of a team's goal threat is calculated based on the responsability model.
details.meaning: Changing your team's goal threat through passive actions. If the opponent manages to get the ball into our box, he not only increases his own goal threat but also decreases the opponent's goal threat. This drop in the threat of conceding goals due to passive behaviour is distributed among the players of your own team based on the responsiveness model.

details.label: Other
details.definition: The change in a team's goal threat due to actions that can't be attributed to a player. It consists of 'OPP_PXT_SHOT' and 'OPP_PXT_NOVIDEO'.
details.meaning: Changes of a team's goal threat through 'other' actions.

details.label: Fouled
details.definition: The change in a team's goal threat which is achieved by being fouled.
details.meaning: Changing your team's goal threat by getting fouled. The highest scores are achieved by winning a penalty.

details.label: Active actions (Defensive Threat)
details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding by active actions like passes, dribbles, set pieces and blocks.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by active actions on the ball (passes, dribbles, set pieces blocks). If a player only has successful progressive passes, he is only increases the own team's goal threat but also descreasing the opponent team's goal threat.

details.label: Other (Defensive Threat)
details.definition: The change in the threat of conceding by other actions. This KPI consists of 'PXT_FOUL' and 'PXT_NO_VIDEO' and is attributed to all players of the opponent team.
details.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding through 'other' actions.

details.label: Number of presses
details.definition: A player applies a 'press' if (1) he is attacking the ball carrying player with the intention to win the ball or (2) he is closer than 1m to the ball carrying player.
details.meaning: Applying a high amount of pressure on the opponent player.

details.label: Number of presses during opponent build-up
details.definition: Number of presses on the opponent's passes when the opposition is in the possession phase from the defensive zones (CB & FB zones).
details.meaning: Applying a high amount of pressure on the opponent player during build-up.

details.label: Number of presses between the lines
details.definition: Number of presses on the opponent's ball receptions and passes, when the opponent is in the central midfield zones (DM, CM & AM zones) when the opposition is in the 'in possession' phase.
details.meaning: Applying a high amount of pressure on the opponent player during between the lines actions.

details.label: Number of presses in counter press
details.definition: Number of presses on the opponent's passes, when the opposition is in attacking transition phase.
details.meaning: Applying a high amount of pressure on the opponent player during counter presses.

details.label: Second Ball Starts
details.definition: The action that results in a 'second ball' phase where a high amount of pressure on the ball carrying player is applied and where no team has real control over the ball. It is typically the result of a high pass into an aerial duel, a blocked pass/shot or a saved pass or shot.
details.meaning: Action that is the start of a 'second ball' phase.

details.label: Second Ball Wins
details.definition: The win of the 'second ball' phase is achieved if a player manages to bring enough control over the ball (pressure <40).
details.meaning: Won second ball - bringing the ball under own control after a second ball phase.

details.label: Distance Covered From Deep Runs
details.definition: Difference in the distance to the opposing goal between the reception of a progressive a pass with the action 'availablity from deep runs', and the distance to goal of the previous pass.
details.meaning: Distance to goal covered by receiving a pass in a deep run.

details.label: Distance Covered By Dribbles
details.definition: Difference in the distance to the opposing goal due to the dribble (between release of the ball and reception).
details.meaning: Distance to goal covered by progressive dribbles.

parentKpi.label: Ball Loss Removed Teammates
parentKpi.name: BALL_LOSS_REMOVED_TEAMMATES
parentKpi.definition: A removed teammate is defined as a player who is further away from his own goal than the ball after a loss in possession. If a centre-back plays the ball that is intercepted by the opposing striker who is now free in front of goal, the centre-back has removed 10 of his teammates from the game by losing the ball, as none of them are between the ball and the goal.
parentKpi.meaning: Losing the ball in dangerous & vulnerable areas, turnovers in the build-up stage, enabling the opponent to counterattack.

context.label: Dribble
context.name: DRIBBLE
context.definition: The action dribbling is defined by a player carrying the ball.
context.meaning: Dribbling, carrying the ball, holding the ball

parentKpi.label: Ball Loss Removed Teammates Defenders
parentKpi.name: BALL_LOSS_REMOVED_TEAMMATES_DEFENDERS
parentKpi.definition: Sum of teammate defenders removed from the game. Defender = last 5 players in the given scenario.
parentKpi.meaning: High risk of ball losses that can result in goals conceded, turnovers in the build-up phase, enabling the opponent to counterattack.

context.label: Dribble
context.name: DRIBBLE
context.definition: The action dribbling is defined by a player carrying the ball.
context.meaning: Dribbling, carrying the ball, holding the ball

details.label: PXT Attack
details.definition: Influence on the change in one's own goal threat while the team is in the attacking phase.
details.meaning: Evaluates a player's impact on goal probability while their team is attacking.

details.label: PXT Defend
details.definition: Influence on the change in one's own goal threat while the team is in the defending phase.
details.meaning: Evaluates a player's impact on goal probability while their team is defending.

details.label: DEF PXT Attack
details.definition: Influence on the change in the opponent's goal threat while the team is in the attacking phase.
details.meaning: Evaluates a player's impact on the probability of conceding a goal while their team is attacking.

details.label: DEF PXT Defend
details.definition: Influence on the change in the opponent's goal threat while the team is not in the attacking phase.
details.meaning: Evaluates a player's impact on the probability of conceding a goal while their team is defending.

details.label: Yellow Card
details.definition: Yellow cards from your own team.
details.meaning: Number of Yellow Cards.

details.label: Second Yellow Card
details.definition: Second Yellow cards from your own team.
details.meaning: Number of sendings off due to Second Yellow Cards.

parentKpi.label: Dribbles
parentKpi.name: PXT_DRIBBLE
parentKpi.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat with dribbling. This includes all progressive dribbles (increasing the goal threat), regressive dribbles (dribbles without losing the ball but still reduce the goal threat) and unsuccessful dribbles (dribbles that end up with the ball being lost to the opponent or go out of play or when the dribbling player commits a foul themselves). These 3 types of dribbles can also be viewed separately via the KPIs PXT_DRIBBLE_PRO, PXT_DRIBBLE_REG and PXT_DRIBBLE_FAIL.
parentKpi.meaning: Changing your goal threat through dribbling.

context.label: Offensive
context.name: ATTACK
context.definition: Derived from the current attacking squad, this phase describes that the player's team is currently attacking.
context.meaning: The player's team is in the attacking phase or just lost control of the ball and the team that won the ball is facing high pressure by the player's team.

parentKpi.label: Fouled
parentKpi.name: PXT_FOULED
parentKpi.definition: The change in a team's goal threat which is achieved by being fouled.
parentKpi.meaning: Changing your team's goal threat by getting fouled. The highest scores are achieved by winning a penalty.

context.label: Offensive
context.name: ATTACK
context.definition: Derived from the current attacking squad, this phase describes that the player's team is currently attacking.
context.meaning: The player's team is in the attacking phase or just lost control of the ball and the team that won the ball is facing high pressure by the player's team.

parentKpi.name: PXT_REC
parentKpi.definition: The increase in a team’s goal threat by receiving the ball (only positive changes). PXT_REC is not a direct part of the pxT model, because in the pxT model the passer or the set-piece taking player gets the credit for the successful pass/set-piece. We created this KPI anyway so that you can split the increase in goal threat between passer/pass receiver depending on your preference. PXT_REC is therefore the counterpart to PXT_PASS_PRO and PXT_SETPIECE_PRO.
parentKpi.meaning: Increasing your goal threat through receiving.

context.label: Offensive
context.name: ATTACK
context.definition: Derived from the current attacking squad, this phase describes that the player's team is currently attacking.
context.meaning: The player's team is in the attacking phase or just lost control of the ball and the team that won the ball is facing high pressure by the player's team.

parentKpi.label: Set pieces
parentKpi.name: PXT_SETPIECE
parentKpi.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat through set-pieces (free kick, corner, throw-in, goal kick, kick-off). This includes all progressive set-pieces (increasing the goal threat), regressive set-pieces (passes that reach a teammate but reduce goal threat) and set-pieces that end up with possession lost to the opponent or offside. These 3 types of set-pieces can also be viewed separately via the KPIs PXT_SETPIECE_PRO, PXT_SETPIECE_REG and PXT_SETPIECE_FAIL.
parentKpi.meaning: Changing your goal threat through set-pieces.

context.label: Offensive
context.name: ATTACK
context.definition: Derived from the current attacking squad, this phase describes that the player's team is currently attacking.
context.meaning: The player's team is in the attacking phase or just lost control of the ball and the team that won the ball is facing high pressure by the player's team.

parentKpi.label: Shots
parentKpi.name: PXT_SHOT
parentKpi.definition: The change in a team’s goal threat through shots. A distinction is made whether the shot was on target or not. The reason for this is that the pxT model is based on post-shot xG: If the shot is on target, PXT_SHOT is calculated by the difference between post-shot xG and the goal threat immediately before the shot. If the shot is not on target (if it is blocked or does not hit the target), PXT_SHOT is calculated by the difference between the goal threat after the shot and the goal threat before the shot.
parentKpi.meaning: Changing your goal threat through shots.

context.label: Offensive
context.name: ATTACK
context.definition: Derived from the current attacking squad, this phase describes that the player's team is currently attacking.
context.meaning: The player's team is in the attacking phase or just lost control of the ball and the team that won the ball is facing high pressure by the player's team.

parentKpi.label: Ball losses (Defensive Threat)
parentKpi.name: DEF_PXT_BALL_LOSS
parentKpi.definition: The change in the threat of conceding by losing the ball actively.
parentKpi.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by ball losses (active KPI).

context.label: Defensive
context.name: DEFEND
context.definition: Derived from the current attacking squad, this phase describes that the player's team is currently defending.
context.meaning: The player's team is in the defending phase or just won control of the ball and is facing high pressure by the previously attacking team.

parentKpi.label: Opponent dribbles (Defensive Threat)
parentKpi.name: DEF_PXT_DRIBBLE
parentKpi.definition: The change in the threat of conceding through dribbles from the opponent (progressive dribbles and regressive dribbles). The change in the threat of conceding is distributed to the responsible players based on the responsability model. The responsible players are rewarded for regressive dribbles by the opponent and penalized for progressive dribbles.
parentKpi.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by opponent dribbles.

context.label: Defensive
context.name: DEFEND
context.definition: Derived from the current attacking squad, this phase describes that the player's team is currently defending.
context.meaning: The player's team is in the defending phase or just won control of the ball and is facing high pressure by the previously attacking team.

parentKpi.name: DEF_PXT_FOUL
parentKpi.definition: The change in the threat of conceding due to a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Changing the threat of conceding by fouls. The highest values are achived by fouling within the own box.

context.label: Defensive
context.name: DEFEND
context.definition: Derived from the current attacking squad, this phase describes that the player's team is currently defending.
context.meaning: The player's team is in the defending phase or just won control of the ball and is facing high pressure by the previously attacking team.

details.label: Shot xG from Passes
details.definition: Shot xG from Passes measures the total expected goals (xG) generated from shots that directly followed a player’s passes. The model does not estimate probabilities itself — it simply assigns the xG value of each shot to the pass that created it. All passes that directly lead to a shot are included; passes that don’t result in a shot are ignored.
details.meaning: Shot xG from Passes shows how much scoring potential a player has created through passing. It reflects the quality of chances set up by a player — not just how many — and is unaffected by whether the shot resulted in a goal. It answers the question: “How many expected goals have resulted from this player’s passes?”. This allows us to identify players who consistently create high-value chances, even if their teammates fail to score. Unlike traditional assist stats, this metric focuses on chance quality, not finishing.

details.label: Shot-Creating Actions
details.definition: Shot-Creating Actions are the two offensive actions directly leading to a shot attempt. This includes passes, and fouls drawn that set up a teammate’s shot. Specifically, an Shot-Creating Actions consists of the last two events before a shot, regardless of whether they occur in open play or from set pieces.
details.meaning: Shot-Creating Actions measure the creative involvement of players in shot development, going beyond just assists. They highlight players who consistently contribute to offensive build-up and shot creation, making it a valuable KPI for evaluating playmakers, wingers, and midfielders involved in attacking sequences.

details.label: Won Ground Duels Defensive
details.definition: Won Ground Duels Defensive measures the number of ground duels a player has won in defensive situations, based on the context of the duel (e.g. location, phase of play, team out of possession). Only duels where the player is defending — such as contesting the ball to prevent progression, break up an attack, or regain possession - are counted. It is a subcategory of the broader Won Ground Duels KPI, which includes both offensive and defensive contexts.
details.meaning: Won Ground Duels Defensive highlights a player’s ability to win challenges on the ground while defending. It reflects defensive strength in 1v1 situations, especially when the team is under pressure or out of possession. It answers the question: “How effective is this player at stopping opponents on the ground?” This KPI is particularly useful for evaluating defensive midfielders, center-backs, and pressing forwards - players whose job it is to disrupt attacks and win back control.

details.label: Won Ground Duels Offensive
details.definition: Won Ground Duels Offensive measures the number of ground duels a player has won in offensive situations, based on the phase of play, field position, and team possession status. Only duels where the player is attacking - such as attempting to retain or advance possession under pressure - are counted. It is a subcategory of the broader Won Ground Duels KPI, which includes all contexts.
details.meaning: Won Ground Duels Offensive shows how effective a player is at holding onto the ball or advancing play in contested situations. It reflects ball security, strength, and dribbling ability under pressure. It answers the question: “How successful is this player at winning ground duels while trying to progress the attack?”. This KPI is especially relevant for attacking midfielders, wingers, and forwards who frequently operate in tight spaces and need to overcome defensive pressure to create chances.

details.label: Lost Ground Duels Defensive
details.definition: Lost Ground Duels Defensive measures the number of ground duels lost by a player in defensive situations, such as when the team is out of possession or under pressure. These are duels where the player fails to dispossess the opponent or prevent progression during a defensive action. Only duels classified as defensive based on phase of play, location, and context are included.
details.meaning: This KPI reflects a player’s defensive vulnerability in 1v1 ground duels. It answers the question: “How often does this player fail to stop opponents in defensive ground duels?”. A high value may indicate a weakness in defensive dueling, positioning, or timing — particularly relevant for defenders and defensive midfielders. Used in combination with Won Ground Duels Defensive, it provides a complete picture of a player’s defensive ground duel efficiency.

details.label: Lost Ground Duels Offensive
details.definition: Lost Ground Duels Offensive counts the number of ground duels lost by a player in offensive situations, such as when trying to advance the ball, hold off a defender, or dribble past an opponent. These are duels initiated in possession or during attacking play that result in loss of control or dispossession.
details.meaning: This KPI shows how often a player loses the ball in contested offensive ground situations. It answers the question: “How frequently does this player fail to win ground duels while attacking?”. A high number may point to risky decision-making or inefficiency in tight spaces, while a moderate number can reflect an ambitious playstyle. It is especially relevant for wingers, attacking midfielders, and strikers, and becomes more insightful when viewed together with Won Ground Duels Offensive.

details.label: Won Aerial Duels Defensive
details.definition: Won Aerial Duels Defensive measures the number of aerial duels a player has won in defensive situations, based on the context of the duel (e.g. location, phase of play, team out of possession). Only aerial contests where the player is defending - such as clearing long balls, contesting crosses, or preventing aerial progression - are counted. It is a subcategory of the broader Won Aerial Duels KPI, which includes both offensive and defensive contexts.
details.meaning: Won Aerial Duels Defensive highlights a player’s ability to win aerial challenges while defending. It reflects aerial strength, timing, and positioning in 1v1 defensive headers, especially in high-pressure moments such as set-pieces or defensive transitions. It answers the question: “How effective is this player at stopping opponents in the air?”. This KPI is particularly useful for center-backs, defensive midfielders, and full-backs, who are often tasked with defending aerial threats.

details.label: Won Aerial Duels Offensive
details.definition: Won Aerial Duels Offensive measures the number of aerial duels a player has won in offensive situations, based on the phase of play, field position, and team possession status. Only duels where the player is attacking - such as attempting to flick on a ball, win a long pass, or challenge in the final third - are counted. It is a subcategory of the broader Won Aerial Duels KPI.
details.meaning: Won Aerial Duels Offensive shows how effective a player is at winning aerial contests in attacking scenarios. It reflects aerial presence, physicality, and strategic positioning to maintain or advance possession through headers. It answers the question: “How successful is this player at winning aerial duels while progressing the attack?”. This KPI is especially relevant for target forwards, wingers, and attacking midfielders, who often operate under pressure in aerial situations and can influence second-ball outcomes.

details.label: Lost Aerial Duels Defensive
details.definition: Lost Aerial Duels Defensive measures the number of aerial duels lost by a player in defensive situations, such as defending long balls, set-pieces, or crosses while the team is out of possession. These are instances where the player fails to win the aerial challenge, allowing the opponent to retain or advance possession in a defensive context.
details.meaning: This KPI reflects a player’s defensive vulnerability in aerial duels. It answers the question: “How often does this player fail to stop opponents in the air during defensive situations?”. A high number may indicate issues with aerial timing, positioning, or physical presence, and is particularly relevant for players in key defensive roles such as center-backs and defensive midfielders. Combined with Won Aerial Duels Defensive, it provides a full view of aerial effectiveness in the defensive third.

details.label: Lost Aerial Duels Offensive
details.definition: Lost Aerial Duels Offensive counts the number of aerial duels lost by a player in offensive situations, such as challenging for long balls, flick-ons, or contested headers in the attacking half. These are duels initiated while in possession or during attacking play that result in loss of control or failure to advance.
details.meaning: This KPI shows how often a player fails to win aerial contests in attacking situations. It answers the question: “How frequently does this player lose aerial duels while attacking?”. A high value may reflect difficulty in holding the ball under pressure or limited aerial ability, while a moderate number can indicate a player regularly engaging in high-risk aerial challenges. It is especially relevant for strikers, wingers, and offensive midfielders, and becomes more insightful when compared with Won Aerial Duels Offensive.

details.label: Number of Fouls
details.definition: Number of Fouls counts how often a player is recorded as having committed a foul during the match, based on tagging or referee decisions.
details.meaning: This KPI reflects a player’s defensive aggression and physicality. A high number can indicate intense defensive engagement or tactical fouling, but may also point to poor timing or discipline issues. It is often used alongside cards and stoppage data to assess defensive behavior.

details.label: Number of Fouls Resulting in Penalties
details.definition: This KPI counts how often a player commits a foul inside their own penalty area that results in a penalty kick for the opponent.
details.meaning: This metric highlights critical defensive mistakes with high consequences. A high value may suggest poor decision-making under pressure, lack of positional awareness, or excessive aggression in high-risk zones. Particularly important when evaluating defensive reliability and discipline inside the box.

details.label: Number of Fouls Won
details.definition: Number of Fouls Won measures how often a player is fouled by an opponent during the match, based on tagging or referee decisions.
details.meaning: This KPI reflects how often a player draws fouls, often due to technical skill, pressing resistance, or dribbling activity. A high value may suggest that a player is targeted frequently by defenders or is highly active in contested zones. Useful for assessing ball carriers, creative players, or pressing triggers.

details.label: Number of Penalties Won
details.definition: This KPI counts how often a player is fouled inside the opponent’s penalty area, resulting in a penalty kick for their team.
details.meaning: This metric highlights players who create high-impact situations through their movement or dribbling in dangerous areas. A high number indicates offensive threat and the ability to provoke critical defensive errors. Common among forwards, wingers, or attacking midfielders who operate frequently in the box.

details.label: Number of Offsides
details.definition: Number of Offsides counts how often a player is penalized for being in an offside position during the match. This includes situations where the player actively interferes with play or gains an advantage from their position, as recorded by the referee or tagging system.
details.meaning: This KPI reflects a player’s timing and positioning in the attacking phase. A high number may indicate a tendency to play close to the defensive line and take risks to break through, but it can also signal poor timing, lack of awareness, or miscommunication. Commonly used to assess forwards and wide players who frequently make runs behind the defense.

details.label: Ball-in-Play Time
details.definition: Ball-in-Play Time measures the actual time a player was actively on the pitch during live play - excluding stoppages, substitutions, and inactive phases. It reflects the effective game time that the player was eligible to influence the match, typically calculated by subtracting non-playing phases (e.g. time on the bench, injury breaks, half-time) from total match duration.
details.meaning: Ball-in-Play Time indicates how much real match time a player had to impact the game. It allows for fair comparisons between players who played different amounts of minutes due to substitutions or interruptions. It answers the question: “How many minutes of active football did this player actually contribute to?”. This KPI is especially useful when analyzing per-minute metrics, ensuring that player stats are normalized to effective presence, not just total match duration.

details.label: Pre Assist
details.definition: The Pre-Assist measures the number of passes or actions that directly precede an assist. It captures the contribution of the player who delivers the second-last action before a goal, typically the pass that sets up the assist. This KPI helps identify players who play a key role in goal creation without being the direct assister. Only open-play and set-piece scenarios are considered - secondary assists resulting from rebounds or deflections are not counted.
details.meaning: Pre-Assists highlight the creative and tactical influence of players who are heavily involved in build-up play. They often indicate vision, awareness, and passing quality. This KPI is especially useful for evaluating playmakers or midfielders whose impact on goal-scoring goes beyond traditional assists.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Low Pass
context.name: LOW_PASS
context.definition: A flat pass with the foot is when the trajectory of the ball during a pass reaches a maximum of chest height. The ball can land with a teammate, an opponent or out of bounds.
context.meaning: A flat pass with the foot.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Diagonal Pass
context.name: DIAGONAL_PASS
context.definition: A diagonal ball is a high ball played with the foot from the centre or out wide and switches of play to the wing. It is distinguished from the chipped/ lofted pass (as this is usually along the line or through the centre), the cross (usually into the box), a clearance (usually under very high pressure) and the short aerial pass (usually a short high passes).
context.meaning: High pass with the foot that changes play to the other side.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Chipped/Lofted Pass
context.name: CHIPPED_PASS
context.definition: A chipped or lofted pass is a high pass made from the foot where the ball travels a distance of at least 15 metres. These are mainly high passes along the line or in the centre of the pitch. It is therefore distinguished from the diagonal ball (usually out to the outside of the pitch), the high cross (usually into the box), a clearance (usually under very high pressure) and a short aerial pas (usually with pass length less than 15 metres).
context.meaning: High pass with the foot with a minimum length of 15 metres.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Short Aerial Pass
context.name: SHORT_AERIAL_PASS
context.definition: A short aerial pass is a high pass from the foot. These are predominantly short lobs, volleys or chips, e.g. as a result of a throw-in or after a bounce. It differs from the chipped/lofted pass primarily due to the length of the pass (<15 metres).
context.meaning: High pass with the foot with a maximum length of 15 metres.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Low Cross
context.name: LOW_CROSS
context.definition: A low cross is a cross into the opponent's box from wide. The ball must not reach more than chest height during it's trajectory, otherwise it is then counted as a high cross.
context.meaning: A low cross is a cross that is a low ball into the opponent's box from the wings.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: High Cross
context.name: HIGH_CROSS
context.definition: A high cross is an aerial cross into the opponent's box from wide. The ball must reach chest height at least once during the ball’s trajectory.
context.meaning: A high cross is a cross that is a high ball into the opponent's box from the wings.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Clearance with Foot
context.name: CLEARANCE
context.definition: A clearance is characterised by the need to relieve a high press situation by clearing the ball with a defensive action. These are most frequently clearances with only one contact on the ball.
context.meaning: A defensive action on the ball with the foot when under high pressure

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Header
context.name: HEADER
context.definition: The action header includes all receptions and releases of the ball that are made with the head. This includes passes with the head and goal finishes with the head.
context.meaning: Header

parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Goal Kick

context.definition: Set piece situation where the ball is put into play by a goal kick.
context.meaning: A player takes a goal kick

parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Full-Back Right Zone
context.name: FBR
context.definition: The RB zone is adjacent to the CB zone and is defined by the player's position being outside of the opponent's formation. To the front, the RB zone borders on the wide right zone. The decisive factor is the number of opponents between the ball and the opposition goal.
context.meaning: Deep zone to the right of the CB zone where the outside right player in the defensive line is usually located.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Centre-Back Zone
context.name: CB
context.definition: The CB zone is located in front of the opposing forwards, usually with all opponents between the ball and the opposing goal. It borders laterally the two FB zones, depending on whether one is inside or outside the opponent's formation. To the front, the CB zone borders on the DM zone, to the back on the GK zone.
context.meaning: Neutral zone in which play is built from the centre.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Full-Back Left Zone
context.name: FBL
context.definition: The LB zone is adjacent to the CB zone and is defined by the player's position being outside of the opponent's formation. To the front, the LB zone borders on the wide left zone. The decisive factor is the number of opponents between the ball and the opposition goal.
context.meaning: Deep zone to the left of the CB zone where the outside left player in the defensive line is usually located.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Defensive-Mid Zone
context.name: DM
context.definition: The DM zone is the central and semi-central zone behind the opposing strikers and in front of the opposing midfielders. It borders the back of the CB zone and the front of the CM zone. It is bordered to the sides by the two wide zone.
context.meaning: Centrally ahead of the opponent strikers/ first line of defence

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Wide Right Zone
context.name: WR
context.definition: The wide-right zone is behind the opposing strikers and often also behind the opposing winger. It is the space to the outside behind the first defensive line and in front of the last defensive line of the opponent. Horizontally, you are outside the opponent's formation. Behind, the zone borders the RB zone and in front the in-behind right zone. Laterally, it borders both the DM zone and the CM zone & can at time also border the AM zone, depending on the opponent's formation & shape.
context.meaning: Space on the outside right behind the first line of defence and in front of the last line of defence of the opponent.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Wide Left Zone
context.name: WL
context.definition: The wide-left zone is behind the opposing strikers and often also behind the opposing winger. It is the space to the outside behind the first defensive line and in front of the last defensive line of the opponent. Horizontally, you are outside the opponent's formation. Behind, the zone borders the LB zone and in front the in-behind left zone. Laterally, it borders both the DM zone and the CM zone & can at time also border the AM zone, depending on the opponent's formation & shape.
context.meaning: Space on the outside left behind the first line of defence and in front of the last line of defence of the opponent.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Centre-Mid Zone
context.name: CM
context.definition: The CM zone is the central and semi-central space within the opponent's midfield, located ahead of the DM zone & behind the AM zone. Vertically, it is located behind the opposing strikers and first midfielders, but not yet directly in front of the last line of defence. Horizontally, it is located between the two wings of the opponent's formation.
context.meaning: Central space ahead of the DM zone and before the AM zone.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Attacking-Mid Zone
context.name: AM
context.definition: The AM zone is the central and semi-central zone immediately in front of the opponent's last line of defence. It is bordered to the back by the CM zone & to the front by the in-behind central zone. Horizontally, it is located inside the opponent's formation. Laterally, it borders the in-behind wide zones.
context.meaning: Central zone directly in front of the opponent's defensive line.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: In-Behind Wide Right Zone
context.name: IBWR
context.definition: The in-behind right zone is behind or next to the opponent's defensive line. At least two opposing centre-backs can still defend the goal, at least one full-back has been bypassed. To the right side, the zone borders the AM zone and the in-behind central zone. From behind it borders the wide right zone.
context.meaning: Tends to be a wide area with at least one full back bypassed.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: In-Behind Wide Left Zone
context.name: IBWL
context.definition: The in-behind left zone is behind or next to the opponent's defensive line. At least two opposing centre-backs can still defend the goal, at least one full-back has been bypassed. To the left side, the zone borders the AM zone and the in-behind central zone. From behind it borders the wide left zone.
context.meaning: Tends to be a wide area with at least one full back bypassed.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: In-Behind Central Zone
context.name: IB
context.definition: The in-behind central zone is the area behind the opponent's defence. An action here often represents a complete breakthrough on goal. There is a maximum of one opposing centre-back and the opposing goalkeeper between the ball and the goal. Vertically, you are within the boundaries of the opponent's formation. Laterally, the zone reaches the two furthest points of the defence (generally each full-back). To the rear, it borders the AM zone.
context.meaning: Tends to be a central space with at least one central defender bypassed.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Goalkeeper Zone
context.name: GK
context.definition: The goalkeeper zone is determined solely by the player position of the goalkeeper. Horizontal and vertical points are not relevant here.
context.meaning: When the goalkeeper has the ball - regardless of where exactly.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes

context.label: First Third
context.name: FIRST_THIRD
context.definition: The first third of the pitch as seen by the team currently in possession of the ball. The box is not included here.
context.meaning: First third of the pitch without the box.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.definition: The half-space on the right from the point of view of the team currently in possession of the ball. This lane is bordered on the right by the edge of the box & to the left by the 6-yard-box.
context.meaning: Right half-space.

context.label: In Possession Phase
context.name: IN_POSSESSION
context.definition: In Possession phase starts when a team has the ball in a zone at the back (GK, FB or CB zone) for at least 4 seconds. The possession phase ends when the opponent wins the ball, there is a second ball, a set-piece follows, or there is any other interruption in play.
context.meaning: Controlled build-up of an attack.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Attacking Transition Phase
context.name: ATTACKING_TRANSITION
context.definition: Attacking Transition phases of play start when possession of the ball is won where there has been some control of the ball (pressure <40). It ends as soon as the team decides to take speed out of the situation (see possession phase), there is a second ball, a set-piece follows or there is any other interruption to play.
context.meaning: Direct attack after winning the ball without having brought the ball under complete control.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Defensive Transition Phase
context.name: DEFENSIVE_TRANSITION
context.definition: The game phase "Defensive Transition" is the counterpart to the game phase "Attacking Transition". If the attacking team is in a switching phase after winning the ball, then the opposing team is in the "defensive transition phase".
context.meaning: Direct defending after losing the ball without the opponent having gained complete control of the ball.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Out of Possession Phase
context.name: OUT_OF_POSSESSION
context.definition: The phase of play "Out of Possession" is the counterpart to the phase of play "In Possession". If the attacking team is in a possession phase, the opposing team is in the "out of possession" phase of play.
context.meaning: Defending against a controlled attack by the opponent.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Set Piece Phase
context.name: SET_PIECE
context.definition: Set piece phases start with the taking of a set piece (throw-in, corner, free kick, goalkeeper kick, penalty kick). While throw-ins, penalty kicks and goalkeeper kicks end after being taken, the set piece phase for corners and free kicks runs until an action with little opponent pressure takes place or the ball has been transported out of the final third. A direct header after a corner therefore also falls into the standard phase.
context.meaning: Attack through a set piece, especially after free kicks and corners.

parentKpi.label: Neutral Passes
parentKpi.name: NEUTRAL_PASSES
parentKpi.definition: A neutral pass is any pass that is received under high pressure (pressure >= 80). This included passes that end in aerial duels or passes that are followed by a foul.
parentKpi.meaning: Pass that ends in a high pressure situation.

context.label: Second Ball Phase
context.name: SECOND_BALL
context.definition: Second Ball phase of the game begins after uncontrollable or extremely hard to control actions, e.g. an aerial duel or blocked pass/shot, when both teams are then in a fight for the ball. You cannot clearly assign possession of the ball to either of the two teams in these phases. The phase ends when one team manages to bring enough control over the ball (pressure <40) or there is a stoppage of play.
context.meaning: Phase in which neither team manages to keep full control of the ball. 'Chaos phase.'

details.label: Shot Assists
details.definition: A shot assist is awarded when a player delivers the final pass or set piece that directly leads to a teammate taking a shot. This includes all types of deliveries such as short or long passes, crosses, through balls, corners, and free kicks, provided the action immediately precedes a shot attempt. The metric is a simple count of such events, independent of whether the resulting shot was on target or resulted in a goal. Only passes to the final third or opponent box are awarded.
details.meaning: SHOT_ASSISTS measure how actively a player contributes to creating shooting opportunities for teammates. A higher number indicates frequent involvement in dangerous situations, showcasing creativity, vision, and execution in both open play and set-piece situations.

details.label: Expected Shot Assists
details.definition: A model-based estimate of how many shot assists a player is expected to produce from their passes or set pieces. For each pass/set piece, the model assigns a probability that it will directly become the final action before a teammate’s shot (per our event-linking rules). EXPECTED_SHOT_ASSISTS is the sum of these probabilities across all passes/set pieces in the selected period, reflecting shot-creation potential based on pass quality.
details.meaning: This KPI provides a lower-variance baseline for creative output than raw SHOT_ASSISTS, indicating how many shot assists a player’s passing would typically yield given the quality of their deliveries. A higher value signals that the player consistently delivers passes/set pieces that tend to generate shots, even if the actual shot-assist count swings from match to match. Comparing SHOT_ASSISTS to EXPECTED_SHOT_ASSISTS helps identify over- or underperformance driven by context (e.g., whether teammates actually took the shot) rather than the passer’s underlying contribution.

details.label: Expected Goal Assists
details.definition: A model-based estimate of how many goal assists a player is expected to record from their passes or set pieces. For each pass/set piece, the model calculates the probability that it will become a shot assist leading to a goal (i.e., an assist), based on the quality of the pass and the typical conversion likelihood. EXPECTED_GOAL_ASSISTS is the sum of these probabilities across all passes/set pieces in the selected period. Only passes and set pieces into the final third or opponent box are awarded.
details.meaning: This KPI provides a stabilized, expectation-based view of creative output, reducing the variance inherent in raw assists. It reflects how many assists a player would normally be expected to achieve given the quality of their deliveries, independent of whether teammates actually finished the chances. A higher value signals that the player consistently generates passes/set pieces with strong goal-assist potential, making it a fairer indicator of underlying creative contribution than realized assists alone.

details.label: Expected Passes
details.definition: A model-based estimate of how many passes and set pieces a player is expected to complete successfully, given the difficulty of each attempted pass. The model evaluates contextual factors such as pass distance, angle, opponents, pressure, distance to goal, distance to side lane, phase of play, action, and attempted receiver positon to assign a completion probability to every attempted pass. EXPECTED_PASSES is the sum of these probabilities across all passes in the selected period.
details.meaning: Expected passes provide a statistical benchmark for assessing passing performance. By comparing expected passes with a player’s actual successful passes, we can measure whether the player is completing more or fewer passes than the model predicts. To do this, two derived scores are useful: Pass Completion Over Expected and Pass Accuracy Over Expected. These scores capture whether a player is performing above, in line with, or below the expected baseline, and help highlight technical passing skill, decision-making, and ability under pressure. Example: If a player attempts 10 passes and completes 8, while the model predicts 7.5 expected completions, then: Pass Completion Over Expected = (8 – 7.5) / 7.5 = +6.7% and Pass Accuracy Over Expected = (8 – 7.5) / 10 = +5%.
